raw_consumer_statement:
I disputed a {$910.00} charge from XXXX for a service renewal that I did not authorize and did not accept. My dispute was based on failure of authorization and material change in terms, not cancellation timing. 

My prior XXXX plan was approximately {$350.00} {$400.00} per year and was fully paid through XX/XX/XXXX. My stored credit card had expired, so auto-renew had already failed. On XX/XX/XXXX, I updated my card only to review options. XXXX immediately charged {$910.00} without displaying the price, presenting a checkout screen, or obtaining my consent. 

XXXX materially changed the product and price by deprecating my prior plan and replacing it with a significantly higher-priced plan. XXXX claims notice of this change was provided by email on XX/XX/XXXX, but neither XXXX nor Citi has provided proof that such notice was delivered, received, or accepted. I did not receive any such email, and no evidence of constructive notice or assent has been produced. 

Despite these issues, Citi denied the dispute solely on the basis of XXXX cancellation policy and did not address the lack of authorization, the material change in product and price, or the absence of any proof of consent or constructive notice. Citi also did not provide documentation showing that I approved the {$910.00} charge. 

I am requesting review of whether Citi properly evaluated this dispute under card network and consumer protection standards, given that the charge involved a material change in terms and was processed without clear disclosure, authorization, or constructive notice.
